sentiment.rationale: Jeremiah's divine call and commission, with God's assurance of protection alongside warnings of opposition from nations.
God indicts Israel for forsaking Him, comparing their apostasy to a bride forgetting her husband and exchanging living water for broken cisterns.
Israel and Judah compared to unfaithful wives, with God pleading for repentance and promising restoration if they return.
A devastating vision of invasion from the north, with the land made desolate and Jeremiah grieving over the destruction.
God searches Jerusalem for even one righteous person; the people have revolted with a stubborn and rebellious heart.
Jerusalem besieged and refined as rejected silver; the people refuse correction and the ancient paths of righteousness.
The temple sermon: stop trusting in the temple as a talisman while practicing injustice and idolatry.
The harvest is past and the people are not saved; Jeremiah weeps over the incurable wound of his people.
Jeremiah wishes his head were waters and his eyes a fountain of tears; mourning over deceit, death, and desolation.
Contrasts the living God with lifeless idols; includes a prayer acknowledging God's incomparability and man's inability to direct his own steps.
The broken covenant recalled; a conspiracy in Judah and a plot against Jeremiah's life in Anathoth.
Jeremiah questions why the wicked prosper; God responds that worse is coming, but also promises restoration for repentant neighbors.
The parable of the ruined linen belt symbolizing Judah's pride and uselessness; warning of exile and shame.
Drought and famine; Jeremiah intercedes but God refuses to relent, condemning the false prophets who promise peace.
Even Moses and Samuel could not intercede for this people; Jeremiah laments his own suffering but receives divine reassurance.
Jeremiah forbidden to marry, mourn, or feast as signs of coming devastation; yet a future gathering is promised.
The deceitful heart, the tree planted by water, and the Sabbath observance; trust in God versus trust in man.
The potter and the clay: God's sovereign right to reshape nations; the people plot against Jeremiah.
The broken flask symbolizes irreversible destruction; Topheth and the valley of slaughter foretold.
Jeremiah beaten and put in stocks by Pashur; his most anguished confession, cursing the day of his birth.
During Nebuchadnezzar's siege, Jeremiah tells Zedekiah that God fights against Jerusalem; surrender or die.
Oracles against the kings of Judah: Shallum, Jehoiakim, and Coniah condemned for injustice and pride.
Woe to false shepherds; the righteous Branch prophecy alongside fierce denunciation of lying prophets.
The vision of two baskets of figs: good figs are the exiles whom God will restore; bad figs are those who stayed behind.
The seventy-year captivity foretold; the cup of God's wrath given to all nations, beginning with Jerusalem.
Jeremiah's temple sermon nearly costs his life; Ahikam saves him, but the prophet Urijah is killed by Jehoiakim.
The yoke of Babylon: God commands nations to submit to Nebuchadnezzar; false prophets contradicted.
Hananiah breaks Jeremiah's wooden yoke and falsely prophesies Babylon's defeat; Jeremiah announces Hananiah's death.
The letter to the exiles: build houses, plant gardens, seek the city's welfare; God's plans for hope and a future.
The Book of Consolation begins: promises of restoration, healing, and return from captivity for Israel and Judah.
The new covenant written on hearts; Rachel weeping comforted; everlasting love proclaimed; the most hopeful chapter in Jeremiah.
Jeremiah buys a field during the siege as a sign that houses and fields will again be bought in the land.
Promises of restoration, the Branch of righteousness, and the perpetuity of the Davidic and Levitical covenants.
Zedekiah's broken covenant regarding freeing Hebrew slaves brings judgment; the people reneged on their pledge.
The Rechabites' faithfulness to their ancestor's commands shames Judah's unfaithfulness to God.
Jehoiakim burns the scroll of Jeremiah's prophecies; Baruch rewrites it with additional words of judgment.
Jeremiah imprisoned for alleged desertion; warns Zedekiah that the Chaldeans will return and burn Jerusalem.
Jeremiah thrown into a muddy cistern; Ebed-melech rescues him; secret counsel with Zedekiah who fears his own nobles.
Jerusalem falls to Babylon; Zedekiah captured and blinded; Jeremiah freed and Ebed-melech protected by God's promise.
Gedaliah appointed governor over the remnant; people begin to gather and harvest; a brief period of fragile peace.
Ishmael assassinates Gedaliah and massacres pilgrims; Johanan pursues but Ishmael escapes to Ammon.
The remnant asks Jeremiah to inquire of God, who commands them to stay in Judah; if they flee to Egypt, sword and famine follow.
The people reject God's word and take Jeremiah to Egypt; he prophesies Nebuchadnezzar will conquer Egypt too.
In Egypt, Jeremiah condemns ongoing idolatry; the people defiantly vow to continue burning incense to the queen of heaven.
A brief personal oracle to Baruch: God acknowledges his grief but warns against seeking great things for himself in a time of judgment.
Oracle against Egypt: Pharaoh Necho's defeat at Carchemish and coming Babylonian invasion; but Israel will be saved.
Oracle against the Philistines: waters rising from the north, the sword of the Lord unsheathed.
Extended oracle against Moab: destruction of its cities and pride; Moab's complacency like undisturbed wine will be poured out.
Oracles against Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Kedar, Hazor, and Elam; none escapes God's judgment.
Oracle against Babylon: its fall foretold; Israel and Judah will seek God and return to Zion weeping.
Babylon's destruction elaborated; the golden cup that made nations drunk will be broken; Seraiah to sink the scroll in the Euphrates.
Historical epilogue: the fall of Jerusalem, destruction of the temple, exile of the people, and the release of Jehoiachin from prison.

missingCharacters.name: Baruch
missingCharacters.description: Son of Neriah, Jeremiah's faithful scribe who wrote and read the prophet's oracles and accompanied him to Egypt.

missingCharacters.name: Ebed-melech
missingCharacters.description: An Ethiopian eunuch in King Zedekiah's court who rescued Jeremiah from the muddy cistern and was promised divine protection.

missingCharacters.name: Pashur
missingCharacters.description: Son of Immer, chief governor of the temple, who struck Jeremiah and put him in stocks for prophesying Jerusalem's destruction.

missingCharacters.name: Jehoiachin
missingCharacters.description: Son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah who reigned three months before being exiled to Babylon; later released from prison by Evil-merodach.

missingCharacters.name: Seraiah
missingCharacters.description: Son of Neriah, brother of Baruch, a quartermaster who carried Jeremiah's scroll of doom against Babylon to the Euphrates.

missingCharacters.name: Elnathan
missingCharacters.description: Son of Achbor, a prince of Judah who urged Jehoiakim not to burn Jeremiah's scroll but was ignored.

missingCharacters.name: Irijah
missingCharacters.description: Captain of the guard at the Benjamin Gate who arrested Jeremiah on false charges of deserting to the Chaldeans.

missingCharacters.name: Hanamel
missingCharacters.description: Son of Shallum, Jeremiah's cousin who sold him the field at Anathoth as a sign of future restoration.

summaries.summary: God calls Jeremiah as a prophet before his birth, appointing him over nations; visions of an almond rod and a boiling pot signal watchfulness and invasion from the north.
God recounts Israel's early faithfulness and indicts them for forsaking the fountain of living waters to hew broken cisterns that hold nothing.
Israel and Judah are likened to unfaithful wives; God pleads for their return, promising restoration and true shepherds in Zion.
A call to repentance is followed by a terrifying vision of invasion from the north, with the land returning to primordial chaos.
God searches Jerusalem for one just person and finds none; the people deny God, and a distant nation will devour their harvest.
Jerusalem is besieged and warned; the people refuse the ancient paths of righteousness and are rejected like impure silver.
Jeremiah delivers the temple sermon, warning that the temple will not protect a people who steal, murder, and burn incense to Baal.
The dead will be dishonored, the living persist in backsliding, and Jeremiah grieves that there is no balm in Gilead to heal his people.
Jeremiah weeps for the slain; God declares He will scatter the people because they forsook the law and followed the Baals.
A contrast between the living God who made heaven and earth and the worthless idols fashioned by human hands; Jeremiah prays for correction with justice.
God recalls the covenant from Egypt and charges Judah with conspiracy to return to their ancestors' sins; the men of Anathoth plot against Jeremiah.
Jeremiah questions why the wicked prosper; God answers that if he cannot contend with footmen, how will he face horsemen, and promises judgment and eventual mercy.
The parable of the ruined linen belt and the vision of full wineflasks illustrate Judah's ruined pride and coming intoxication of judgment.
A severe drought afflicts the land; Jeremiah intercedes but God refuses to relent, denouncing false prophets who promise peace.
Even Moses and Samuel could not save this people; Jeremiah laments his suffering and receives assurance that God will deliver him.
Jeremiah is forbidden to marry, mourn, or feast as signs of coming desolation, yet God promises a future exodus greater than from Egypt.
The heart is deceitful above all things; blessed is the man who trusts in God like a tree by waters; Sabbath observance is commanded.
At the potter's house, Jeremiah learns that God reshapes nations like clay; the people plot against Jeremiah and he cries out for justice.
Jeremiah breaks an earthen flask in the valley of Hinnom to symbolize the irreversible shattering of Jerusalem and its people.
Pashur strikes Jeremiah and puts him in stocks; Jeremiah delivers an oracle against Pashur, then pours out his most anguished lament, cursing the day of his birth.
During the Babylonian siege, Jeremiah tells Zedekiah that God Himself fights against Jerusalem; the only way to live is to surrender.
Oracles against the kings of Judah: Shallum will never return, Jehoiakim will have a donkey's burial, and Coniah is cast off.
Woe to the shepherds who scatter the flock; God will raise a righteous Branch; false prophets condemned for speaking visions of their own hearts.
After Jehoiachin's deportation, God shows Jeremiah two baskets of figs: the good exiles He will restore, and the bad remnant He will destroy.
After twenty-three years of unheeded warning, Jeremiah proclaims seventy years of Babylonian captivity and the cup of God's wrath for all nations.
Jeremiah is seized and tried for prophesying the temple's destruction; the princes and people spare him, recalling the precedent of Micah.
Jeremiah wears a yoke and tells the ambassadors that God has given all nations to Nebuchadnezzar; do not listen to prophets who say otherwise.
The false prophet Hananiah breaks Jeremiah's wooden yoke and promises Babylon's defeat within two years; Jeremiah announces Hananiah will die that year.
Jeremiah's letter to the Babylonian exiles: build houses, plant gardens, seek the city's welfare, for after seventy years God will restore them with plans for hope and a future.
The Book of Consolation opens with promises of deliverance: Jacob's trouble will end, his wounds will be healed, and the city rebuilt.
God proclaims everlasting love, Rachel's weeping is comforted, and a new covenant is promised in which the law will be written on hearts.
While imprisoned, Jeremiah buys his cousin Hanamel's field at Anathoth as a sign that fields and vineyards will again be bought in the land.
God promises to restore Jerusalem, raise the Branch of righteousness, and establish perpetual covenants with the house of David and the Levites.
Zedekiah and the people make a covenant to free Hebrew slaves, then recapture them; God declares they have profaned His name and will face destruction.
The Rechabites' loyalty to their ancestor Jonadab's command to abstain from wine shames Judah's persistent disobedience to God.
Baruch writes and reads Jeremiah's scroll in the temple; King Jehoiakim burns it column by column, and a second scroll is written with additional judgments.
During a temporary Babylonian withdrawal, Jeremiah warns the Chaldeans will return; he is arrested, accused of desertion, and imprisoned.
Jeremiah is thrown into a muddy cistern to die; Ebed-melech the Ethiopian rescues him; Zedekiah secretly consults Jeremiah but is too afraid to surrender.
Jerusalem falls to Babylon; Zedekiah is captured and blinded; Nebuzaradan frees Jeremiah, and Ebed-melech is spared for trusting God.
Nebuzaradan releases Jeremiah to join Gedaliah, the appointed governor; scattered Jews gather at Mizpah, and Johanan warns of Ishmael's plot.
Ishmael assassinates Gedaliah and murders Babylonian soldiers and pilgrims; Johanan rescues the captives but Ishmael escapes to Ammon.
The remnant asks Jeremiah to pray for direction; after ten days God says to stay in Judah, warning that fleeing to Egypt means death.
The people reject God's command and flee to Egypt, taking Jeremiah with them; he prophesies Nebuchadnezzar will set his throne there too.
In Egypt, Jeremiah confronts the people's continued worship of the queen of heaven; they defiantly refuse to stop, and God pronounces destruction.
A brief oracle to Baruch from the fourth year of Jehoiakim: God acknowledges Baruch's sorrow but warns him not to seek great things for himself.
Oracle against Egypt: Pharaoh Necho's army is shattered at Carchemish, and Egypt will be invaded by Nebuchadnezzar, but Israel will be restored.
Oracle against the Philistines: waters from the north overwhelm Gaza and Ashkelon as the sword of the Lord devours.
An extensive oracle against Moab: its cities are destroyed, its complacent pride is broken, and its wine vats cease, though future restoration is mentioned.
Oracles against Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Kedar, Hazor, and Elam, proclaiming God's judgment on surrounding nations.
Babylon's fall proclaimed: her idols shattered, Israel's debt forgiven; the people of God called to flee Babylon and return to Zion.
The destruction of Babylon elaborated: she is the golden cup of the Lord's wrath now shattered; Seraiah sinks the scroll in the Euphrates as a sign.
Historical appendix: the siege and fall of Jerusalem, the destruction of Solomon's temple, the exile, and the release of Jehoiachin from prison in Babylon.

crossReferences.note: God's foreknowledge and foreordination of souls before birth parallels the premortal council.
Jesus quotes this passage when cleansing the temple: 'a den of robbers.'
The natural man and the deceitful heart both describe the fallen human condition requiring divine change.
The righteous Branch raised unto David parallels Book of Mormon messianic prophecies of a future deliverer.
God's plans for welfare and hope parallel the assurance that afflictions shall be for one's good.
Rachel weeping for her children fulfilled in Herod's massacre of the innocents at Bethlehem.
The new covenant passage is quoted at length in Hebrews as fulfilled in Christ.
Paul alludes to the law written on hearts rather than on tablets of stone.
God's promise to reveal great and unsearchable things parallels millennial revelations of hidden knowledge.
Israel and Judah weeping and seeking God in their return parallels the Book of Mormon gathering promises.
Fishers and hunters gathering Israel parallels the keys of the gathering committed to Joseph Smith.
Virtually identical historical account of Jehoiachin's release from prison in Babylon.

historicalContext.setting: Jerusalem, cistern / court of the guard

verses.reason: Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee — foundational scripture on foreordination and the sanctity of life.
God's promise that enemies will not prevail against Jeremiah, for He is with him to deliver.
My people have forsaken the fountain of living waters and hewn broken cisterns — powerful metaphor for apostasy.
God promises pastors after His own heart who will feed with knowledge and understanding.
Jeremiah's anguished cry as he hears the trumpet of war and sees destruction upon destruction.
A people with eyes that see not and ears that hear not — echoed by Christ in the New Testament.
Stand ye in the ways and ask for the old paths, where is the good way, and walk therein.
Is this house become a den of robbers? — quoted by Jesus when cleansing the temple.
Is there no balm in Gilead? Is there no physician there? — a timeless expression of desperate hope.
Let not the wise man glory in his wisdom, nor the mighty in his might, nor the rich in his riches.
But let him that glorieth glory in this: that he understandeth and knoweth me, that I am the LORD.
O LORD, I know that the way of man is not in himself; it is not in man that walketh to direct his steps.
If thou hast run with the footmen and they have wearied thee, then how canst thou contend with horses?
Thy words were found and I did eat them; thy word was unto me the joy and rejoicing of mine heart.
God will send fishers and hunters to find His people — a gathering of Israel prophecy.
Blessed is the man that trusteth in the LORD, and whose hope the LORD is.
The heart is deceitful above all things and desperately wicked — one of the most quoted verses on human nature.
As the clay is in the potter's hand, so are ye in mine hand, O house of Israel.
His word was in mine heart as a burning fire shut up in my bones — the irrepressible call to prophesy.
O earth, earth, earth, hear the word of the LORD — a dramatic triple invocation.
I will raise unto David a righteous Branch — a messianic prophecy of Christ.
He shall be called THE LORD OUR RIGHTEOUSNESS — a divine title of the Messiah.
These nations shall serve the king of Babylon seventy years — the pivotal prophecy of the captivity's duration.
For I know the thoughts that I think toward you, thoughts of peace and not of evil, to give you an expected end.
Ye shall seek me, and find me, when ye shall search for me with all your heart.
For I will restore health unto thee and I will heal thee of thy wounds, saith the LORD.
I have loved thee with an everlasting love; therefore with lovingkindness have I drawn thee.
I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel — the foundational new covenant prophecy.
I will put my law in their inward parts and write it in their hearts — the internalization of God's law.
There is nothing too hard for thee — Jeremiah's confession of God's omnipotence during the siege.
I am the LORD, the God of all flesh: is there any thing too hard for me?
Call unto me, and I will answer thee, and shew thee great and mighty things which thou knowest not.
Seekest thou great things for thyself? Seek them not — a sobering reminder of perspective amid crisis.
Israel and Judah shall come together, going and weeping, and shall seek the LORD their God.
Evil-merodach lifts up the head of Jehoiachin — a faint glimmer of hope at the book's end.
